bull.b: Stryker's materially larger revenue base and sustained ~60–64% gross margins indicate durable scale and pricing power in hospitals and surgical settings. Scale supports R&D, distribution and marketing investments that reinforce competitive position and defend margins over the medium term.

Robust and improving cash generation with multi‑billion FCF provides lasting flexibility to fund acquisitions, product rollouts, dividends and debt paydown. Consistent FCF supports strategic investment without depending on equity markets, strengthening long‑term financial durability.

Strong robotic adoption and new product rollouts (Mako, RPS handheld, Mako 4) create an expanding installed base and recurring consumable/service revenue. Coupled with the Ortho Tech reorganization, this should accelerate commercialization and deepen durable differentiation in orthopaedics.

bear.b: A sustained decline from peak net margins signals structural cost pressures (tariffs, absorption from disruptions, higher interest) that can erode profitability even as revenue grows. If cost headwinds persist, margin recovery may be slow and constrain cash flow expansion.

While leverage has improved, a sizable absolute debt stock makes Stryker exposed to refinancing and rising interest costs, reducing financial flexibility. Higher interest expense can pressure net income and free cash flow, limiting speed of deleveraging or capital deployment during rate shocks.

A major cyber event that disrupted manufacturing, shipments and revenue recognition exposes persistent operational and reputational vulnerabilities. Recovery costs, lost procedures and potential customer trust erosion can have lasting effects on hospital relationships and recurring revenue if not fully mitigated.
